problem_statement: The paper addresses how to make human oversight of AI-based systems in high-risk contexts effective, arguing that reliable detection of errors (inaccurate and unfair outputs) is the foundational precondition for overseers to intervene and mitigate risk.
insufficiency_evidence: "research has cast doubt on whether humans can actually be effective in overseeing systems"; "distinguishing accurate from inaccurate system outputs is challenging even for experts"; people "overwrite accurate system outputs" and "underestimate that systems may produce unfair outputs"
solution_mechanisms: Signal Detection Theory framework for error detection
choosing an explicit standard for (un)fairness as ground truth
cognitive forcing functions to reduce time pressure
deliberately less-reliable systems to keep overseers vigilant
key_constructs: signal detection perspective on error detection
governance_refs: EEOC Uniform Guidelines on Employee Selection (80% rule)
notes: Concerns human oversight of AI-based decision systems (hiring, medical, judicial) generally, not AI-generated code specifically; oversight target is inaccurate/unfair decision outputs rather than code.
